questions.question:
A $1,400\text{ kg}$ sedan travels around a flat highway curve with a radius of $45.0\text{ m}$ at a steady speed of $45.0\text{ km/h}$.

Determine the magnitude of the static friction force acting on the tires. ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)
Planet Z has mass $8.0 \times 10^{24}\text{ kg}$ and radius $8.0 \times 10^6\text{ m}$. Find its surface gravity. ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$)
Two $100\text{ kg}$ masses are $5.0\text{ m}$ apart. What is the gravitational force between them? ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$)
A $70\text{ kg}$ astronaut weighs $686\text{ N}$ on Earth's surface. What is their weight at an altitude of $2R_E$ (3 times Earth's radius from the center)?
questions.explanation: $F \propto 1/r^2$. Tripling $r$ reduces force to $1/9$. Weight $= 686/9 \approx 76.2\text{ N}$.

questions.question: A satellite orbits at radius $2.0 \times 10^7\text{ m}$ with orbital speed $5,000\text{ m/s}$. What is the mass of the planet? ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$)
Planet P orbits a star at radius $r$ with period $T$. Planet Q orbits the same star at radius $4r$. What is Q's period?
The gravitational force between a $60\text{ kg}$ and a $90\text{ kg}$ mass is $1.0 \times 10^{-8}\text{ N}$. How far apart are they? ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$)
Planet X has 3 times Earth's mass and twice Earth's radius. What is its surface gravity in terms of $g_E$?

A $1,400\text{ kg}$ sedan travels around a flat highway curve with a radius of $45.0\text{ m}$ at a steady speed of $45.0\text{ km/h}$.

What is the minimum coefficient of static friction required to keep the car from sliding? ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)
Object A (mass $m$) is at distance $r$ from a planet. Object B (mass $3m$) is at distance $2r$ from the same planet. How does $F_B$ compare to $F_A$?
A planet's mass quadruples while its radius doubles. How does its surface gravity change?
Two satellites of masses $100\text{ kg}$ and $500\text{ kg}$ orbit at the same radius around Earth. How do their orbital speeds compare?
options: The heavier satellite is 5 times faster
The lighter satellite is 5 times faster
Both have the same orbital speed
The heavier satellite is slightly faster
questions.explanation: $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$. Satellite mass cancels out — orbital speed depends only on the central body's mass and radius.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered gravitational and orbital calculations!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the derivations: $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$, $T = 2\pi\sqrt{r^3/(GM)}$, and $g = GM/r^2$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on proportional reasoning with the inverse square law and Kepler's Third Law.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: Newton's Law of Gravitation, orbital speed formula, and how $g$, $F$, $v$, and $T$ vary with $r$.
